output:
# Research Analysis: Augmentation-Marginalized Importance (AMI)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

The field is instance-adaptive data manipulation, or data-centric training: the part of machine learning that decides which examples to emphasize, when to show them, and how to transform them during optimization. Its load-bearing gap is Augmentation-Confounded Importance (ACI). ACI says that the single score used by importance sampling, focal loss, boosting, curriculum learning, self-paced learning, and noisy-label filtering is usually computed on one stochastic view of an example, so it conflates I1, intrinsic difficulty or informativeness; I2, augmentation-induced score variance across crops, masks, mixup partners, token replacements, or other transforms; and I3, example-by-augmentation fragility, where a transformation makes a particular label ambiguous or harmful. Augmentation-Marginalized Importance (AMI) fixes this by treating each example as a distribution over augmented views rather than as one realized view. For example i, AMI estimates the augmentation-marginal mean mu_i of a base score s_i(a) and the across-augmentation fragility variance v_i, then weights the example by mu_i * exp(-lambda v_i) or a reciprocal variant, and adapts per-example augmentation strength tau_i downward when v_i is high. By construction, when K=1 and lambda=0, AMI recovers the original single-view importance sampling, focal, boosting, or curriculum rule as recomputed within floating-point tolerance. AMI therefore strictly contains those prior methods rather than merely competing with them.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Modern deep learning made two data-centric moves at roughly the same time, but the literature usually developed them as separate braids. The first braid asks which training examples should matter most. Classical optimization already suggested that nonuniform sampling can reduce variance: Needell, Ward, and Srebro connected weighted stochastic-gradient updates to randomized Kaczmarz methods [1], and Katharopoulos and Fleuret later made the deep-learning version explicit by sampling examples according to gradient-norm estimates [2]. In parallel, hard-example methods increased weight on cases that the learner currently mishandles. Boosting made this principle canonical by repeatedly shifting mass toward misclassified examples [3], and focal loss adapted the idea to dense detection by down-weighting easy examples and emphasizing high-loss ones [4]. Curriculum and self-paced learning moved in the opposite temporal direction: start with easier or more reliable data, then admit harder samples. Self-paced learning with diversity proposed a regularized selection rule rather than a fixed human curriculum [5]; MentorNet learned a teacher network to produce curricula under corrupted labels [6]; and controlled-noisy-label work made the noise sensitivity of sample-selection rules a central empirical concern [7]. Across these variants, the shared abstraction is a per-example scalar: loss, margin, confidence, gradient norm, teacher score, or boosting weight.

The second braid asks how examples should be transformed. Data augmentation became part of the standard recipe that made large convolutional systems such as AlexNet and ResNet work well on benchmarks including ImageNet and CIFAR-style datasets [18] [19] [20], and the same principle now appears across modalities. Mixup trains on convex combinations of examples and labels [8], while sequence-level mixed-sample augmentation adapted that intuition to text [9]. AutoAugment and FastAutoAugment searched for image transformation policies that optimize validation performance [10] [11]. SpecAugment made masking and warping central to speech recognition [12]. Transformer-based augmentation uses pretrained language models to perturb or generate text while attempting to preserve label semantics [13]. A neighboring robustness braid treats perturbations as adversarial or regularizing transformations rather than ordinary augmentation: PGD adversarial training optimizes against worst-case input perturbations [14], and SMART regularizes fine-tuning of pretrained language models by enforcing smoothness around each input [15]. Modern training stacks also add optimizer and compression choices such as AdamW and TinyBERT-style distillation, showing how much of performance now comes from the data-and-training pipeline rather than architecture alone [21] [22] [23].

The key seam is that these two braids meet operationally even when they are designed conceptually apart. The score used by the first braid is almost never computed on an abstract clean example. It is computed on the actual view seen by the network: a crop, mask, mixup pair, synonym-replaced sentence, acoustic time mask, adversarially perturbed input, or otherwise transformed instance. Thus the scalar that decides which example matters is already a function of the augmentation distribution. A high loss on one crop may mean the object is intrinsically hard, or it may mean that this crop removed the diagnostic part. A large gradient under mixup may mean informative boundary mass, or it may mean that the sampled partner made the label ambiguous. A focal-loss spike may be a useful hard example, a noisy label, or an augmentation artifact.

Existing methods mostly collapse all of these cases into one number. Importance sampling treats the realized gradient or loss as the quantity to sample by [1] [2]. Focal loss and boosting treat current error as useful hardness [3] [4]. Curriculum, self-paced, and MentorNet-style methods may learn more robust schedules, but the basic signal remains a single difficulty or trustworthiness scalar, sometimes supplied by an auxiliary network rather than decomposed from the augmentation process itself [5] [6] [7]. Augmentation-policy methods optimize transformations globally, per dataset, or per class-like regime; they do not ask whether a particular example's importance estimate is stable across its own augmentation distribution [10] [11] [12] [13]. Noise-robust training such as co-teaching filters small-loss samples using peer networks, but again the augmentation-induced variance channel is not the object being measured [16]. Influence-function and uncertainty tools show that per-example signals can be valuable, yet they usually analyze a realized training point or model state, not the distribution of scores induced by stochastic augmentation [17].

This makes Augmentation-Confounded Importance (ACI) the load-bearing gap. ACI says that the per-example importance or difficulty signal used for sampling, weighting, focal emphasis, boosting, or curriculum selection is computed from a single augmented view and therefore conflates intrinsic difficulty, augmentation-induced score variance, and example-by-augmentation fragility. The field has strong tools for deciding which examples matter and strong tools for deciding how to transform examples, but it has not made the importance score itself augmentation-marginal. Augmentation-Marginalized Importance (AMI) fills that seam by estimating, for each example, the augmentation-marginal mean score mu_i and the across-augmentation fragility variance v_i, then using mu_i for clean importance, v_i to cap or penalize fragile/noisy cases, and v_i again to control per-example augmentation strength. With K=1 and lambda=0, AMI collapses exactly to the single-view weighting rule it generalizes.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

The central finding is that AMI turns a familiar practical nuisance into the primary statistical object. Existing weighting methods behave as if a single score s_i(a) is a property of example i. In modern training, however, s_i(a) is a property of example i under a sampled augmentation a: a crop, mask, mixup partner, token replacement, acoustic mask, or adversarial/regularized perturbation. The network therefore sees a single-view control signal that mixes I1, the intended intrinsic informativeness or difficulty; I2, stochastic score variation caused by augmentation; and I3, example-by-augmentation fragility where a particular transformation damages label semantics. ACI is the claim that this mixture is not a harmless measurement detail. It is the control channel used to decide sampling probability, loss weight, curriculum admission, focal emphasis, or noisy-label suppression.

Parker's checkable proposition makes the confound precise. Let s_i(a) be the nonnegative score used for weighting, and let c_i(a) be any scalar contribution such as loss, a projected gradient coordinate, or a norm proxy. The expected single-view weighted contribution differs from the augmentation-marginal weighted contribution by

E_a[s_i(a)c_i(a)] - mu_i E_a[c_i(a)] = Cov_a(s_i(a), c_i(a)).

Thus the single-view rule is unbiased only when the augmentation-induced variation in the score is uncorrelated with the augmentation-induced variation in the contribution. This is a strong condition and is exactly what strong augmentations, ambiguous examples, and noisy labels tend to violate. For normalized sampling, p_i^SV = s_i(a_i) / sum_j s_j(a_j), a first-order delta expansion around S = sum_j mu_j gives

E[p_i^SV] - mu_i / S approx - Cov_a(s_i(a_i), sum_j s_j(a_j)) / S^2,

with the self-term dominated by Var_a[s_i(a_i)] under independent augmentations across examples. The error is therefore not merely high variance in an informal sense; augmentation covariance and augmentation variance move the sampler away from the intended marginal sampler.

AMI's estimator directly targets the missing moments. With K independent views, hat_mu_i = (1/K) sum_k s_i(a_k) and hat_v_i = (1/(K-1)) sum_k (s_i(a_k) - hat_mu_i)^2. Under fixed theta and a fixed augmentation distribution, hat_mu_i is unbiased and Var(hat_mu_i)=v_i/K, while hat_v_i is unbiased for v_i when K > 1. When K views are too expensive, AMI uses a bias-corrected EMA of first and second moments: m_i tracks s_i(a_t), r_i tracks s_i(a_t)^2, hat_mu_i = m_i/(1-beta^t), and hat_v_i = max(0, r_i/(1-beta^t) - hat_mu_i^2). This does not eliminate non-stationarity; it makes the bias-variance tradeoff explicit and measurable.

The AMI weight w_i = mu_i * exp(-lambda v_i), or the conservative w_i = mu_i / (1 + lambda v_i), separates two decisions that previous methods compress into one number. High mu_i means the example is consistently important across its augmentation distribution. High v_i means the example is fragile, noisy, augmentation-sensitive, or ambiguous, so hard-example amplification is risky and tau_i should be softened. The collapse identity is the necessary unit test: with K=1, lambda=0, and tau_i disabled, AMI's weight is exactly the selected single-view baseline's weight, within floating-point tolerance. Without this identity, AMI would be another heuristic; with it, AMI is a strict generalization.

This explains why deep importance sampling, curricula, and focal-style rules often fail to beat uniform sampling despite appealing theory. They optimize a confounded control signal. Uniform sampling can look strong because it refuses to amplify augmentation luck, label noise, or fragile transformations. AMI also unifies several literatures under one signal: mu_i recovers importance sampling, curriculum/self-paced scoring, focal/boosting emphasis, and hard-example selection; v_i supplies a teacher-free reliability and fragility channel for noisy-label filtering; and tau_i uses the same statistics for per-example augmentation-policy control. The research opportunity is not merely better weighting, but a coherent instance-adaptive training interface that asks whether every per-example signal is intrinsic or augmentation-induced.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

The recommendation is to present AMI as a mechanism-reframing paper: define the confound, prove the covariance mechanism, and validate the collapse and variance predictions before claiming accuracy gains.

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**
Can augmentation-confounded importance, in which single-view loss, gradient-norm, focal, boosting, or curriculum scores conflate intrinsic example difficulty with augmentation-induced variance and example-by-augmentation fragility, be replaced by Augmentation-Marginalized Importance that estimates each example's augmentation-marginal mean score mu_i and across-augmentation fragility variance v_i to weight by clean importance while down-weighting fragile or mislabeled examples and adapting augmentation strength, while provably collapsing to ordinary single-view importance sampling, focal weighting, or curriculum selection as recomputed within floating-point tolerance when K=1 and the fragility penalty lambda=0?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**
AMI matters because it gives a concrete explanation for a persistent empirical puzzle: deep importance sampling and curriculum rules often look theoretically attractive but fail to beat uniform sampling reliably in modern networks. Under ACI, that disappointment is expected. The single-view score is not a clean estimate of usefulness; it is contaminated by augmentation luck, label ambiguity, and fragility. Uniform sampling can win simply because it avoids over-reacting to a noisy control signal.

The broader payoff is unification. Importance sampling, curriculum/self-paced selection, focal or boosting-style hard-example emphasis, noisy-label filtering, and augmentation-policy learning all become special cases of managing the same per-example signal under transformations. If mu_i and v_i are useful, improvements transfer across these sub-literatures rather than remaining trapped inside one algorithm family. The practical consequences are immediate: lower-variance sampling, cheaper training from fewer wasted updates, better stability under strong augmentation, and label-noise robustness without paying for an auxiliary MentorNet-like teacher. AMI also supplies a principled per-example augmentation-strength controller: strengthen augmentation where the example is robust, soften it where transformations make the label unstable.

Conceptually, the work would reshape data-centric ML by changing the unit of analysis from an example to an example under its augmentation distribution. The same view could be applied to gradients, margins, influence estimates, uncertainty, calibration errors, or active-learning scores. A paper that establishes this lens would not merely add another weighting heuristic; it would make every per-example training signal ask whether it is intrinsic to the example or an artifact of the stochastic view used to measure it.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**
1. Compute cost. The direct definition of mu_i and v_i needs multiple augmented views per example per update. If K views require K forward/backward passes, AMI is not competitive with uniform training or single-view importance sampling. The engineering challenge is to make moment estimation near-free: (a) store per-example EMA state for s_i and s_i^2 while still using one augmented view per epoch; (b) optionally use K=2 paired views only in selected batches, sharing the same backbone pass shape and amortizing memory; and (c) use cheap scores when full per-example gradient norms are too expensive, for example loss, gradient-norm upper bounds, focal factors, or margin scores. The state cost is O(n) scalars for mu_i/v_i plus optional counts, which is feasible for CIFAR-scale experiments but must be reported for larger datasets.

2. Non-stationarity. The target moments are functions of theta_t, not fixed dataset attributes. Early in training, every example can look hard; later, only ambiguous or mislabeled examples may remain high-loss. A fixed K snapshot is biased because it estimates moments at one model state and then reuses them after the model changes. A naive running mean also fails: without warmup and bias correction, m_i and r_i are initialized too low; without decay, stale early losses dominate; with too-short decay, v_i is noisy. The EMA horizon is therefore a bias-variance knob: long horizons reduce Monte Carlo noise but lag behind training, while short horizons track changes but can recreate the single-view variance AMI is meant to remove.

3. Confounded variance. v_i is a fragility signal, not a causal label. High v_i can mean that random crops remove the object, mixup changes semantics, synonym replacement flips sentiment, the model is uncertain, or the label is wrong. Down-weighting high-v_i examples is plausible because all these cases can make hard-example amplification unsafe, but interpretation is difficult. In noisy-label settings, high v_i may help suppress mislabeled examples; in rare-but-valid minority cases, the same rule could over-suppress informative examples. This is why disaggregated analysis and lambda sweeps are necessary.

4. Feedback with the augmentation policy. AMI also proposes tau_i decreasing in v_i, but changing tau_i changes A, and changing A changes the moments being estimated. This creates a moving-target feedback loop: a high-v_i example receives gentler augmentations, which may lower future v_i, which can then re-strengthen augmentations. The controller must be slow and stop-gradient-like: estimate moments under the current policy, update tau_i with delay or low learning rate, and avoid backpropagating through the sampling/augmentation controller as if it were an ordinary differentiable loss.

5. Why naive alternatives fail.
   - Uniform sampling ignores importance entirely, so it cannot exploit genuinely high-mu_i informative examples.
   - Single-view importance sampling is exactly the confounded estimator: it treats s_i(a) as if it were mu_i and inherits the covariance bias above.
   - Just averaging K views per step estimates mu_i better but is too expensive when K multiplies training cost, and by itself ignores fragility v_i.
   - Just using loss variance as uncertainty notices instability but lacks the collapse anchor, lacks marginal-mean reweighting, and conflates epistemic uncertainty, aleatoric ambiguity, label noise, and augmentation damage without a variance-reduced importance target.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**
The reason is not that the ingredients were missing; it is that each literature made an assumption that hid the seam. Importance-sampling theory, from weighted SGD to gradient-norm sampling, typically assumes a fixed per-example loss or gradient and treats randomness as minibatch sampling noise, not as augmentation-induced score randomness [1] [2]. Curriculum, self-paced learning, focal loss, and boosting intentionally compress difficulty into one scalar, so they have no channel in which augmentation fragility could live [3] [4] [5]. Augmentation search optimized global policies for validation accuracy, while SpecAugment, mixup, and transformer augmentation focused on which transformations improve generalization, not on whether those transformations destabilize the importance signal of a particular example [8] [9] [10] [11] [12] [13]. Noisy-label systems such as MentorNet or co-teaching spend extra model capacity or peer-network machinery to infer reliability implicitly, while AMI asks whether a cheaper explicit variance statistic already separates useful hardness from fragility [6] [16].

The closest near-miss is consistency-regularization and augmentation-robustness work such as Pi-model, temporal ensembling, UDA, FixMatch, and augmentation-consistency or expected-calibration evaluations: these methods do compute agreement or variance across multiple augmented views of the same example, but they use the multi-view signal as a prediction-space regularizer or robustness/quality metric. Prior multi-view work regularizes the model across views; AMI marginalizes the importance signal across views, producing v_i that reweights sampling by augmentation-marginal mu_i, down-weights fragile or mislabeled examples, controls per-example augmentation strength tau_i, and still collapses to ordinary single-view importance sampling when K=1 and lambda=0.

Three barriers kept the idea unsolved. First, compute: naively estimating augmentation variance looks like K forward/backward passes per example, so it appears incompatible with the efficiency motivation behind importance sampling. AMI's answer is to estimate mu_i and v_i nearly free with small K, cached stochastic views, or exponential moving averages across epochs. Second, concept: augmentation was treated as a fixed stochastic layer, a source of free regularization, rather than a measurable per-example distribution whose induced score variance is itself informative. Third, entanglement: without the collapse identity, variance-decomposed weighting looks like just another heuristic. AMI differs by making the augmentation distribution a first-class measured object and by strictly generalizing existing rules: K=1 and lambda=0 recovers ordinary single-view importance sampling, focal weighting, boosting, or curriculum selection as recomputed within floating-point tolerance.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**
### Method

AMI is a wrapper around an existing per-example weighting rule. Choose a base score s_i(a): loss; per-example gradient-norm upper bound following Katharopoulos-Fleuret 2018 [2]; focal modulating factor following Lin et al. 2017 [4]; margin; or another nonnegative single-view importance score. Maintain per-example moment state:

m_i <- beta m_i + (1-beta) s_i(a_t)
r_i <- beta r_i + (1-beta) s_i(a_t)^2
hat_mu_i = bias_correct(m_i)
hat_v_i = max(0, bias_correct(r_i) - hat_mu_i^2)

Use the weight

w_i = hat_mu_i * exp(-lambda * hat_v_i)

with a normalized sampler p_i = w_i / sum_j w_j, or use the reciprocal form w_i = hat_mu_i / (1 + lambda hat_v_i) as a numerically conservative variant. For augmentation strength, define

z(v_i) = standardized rank or clipped z-score of hat_v_i in the current training window
tau_i = clip(tau0 * (1 - alpha * z(v_i)), tau_min, tau_max)

so examples with larger fragility receive gentler augmentations. tau_i is updated slowly, for example with a second EMA or every M steps, and is treated as stop-gradient control rather than a differentiable objective. Training starts with a uniform warmup so every example obtains initial moment estimates before AMI sampling is activated.

Training loop:

1. Warmup for E_w epochs with uniform sampling and standard augmentation; update m_i, r_i, counts, and bias-corrected hat_mu_i/hat_v_i.
2. After warmup, compute w_i from hat_mu_i and hat_v_i; normalize or use a reservoir/alias sampler for minibatch selection.
3. For each selected example i, sample augmentation strength tau_i and augmentation a ~ A(tau_i).
4. Compute the ordinary training loss and optimizer step; do not backpropagate through the sampler or tau_i controller.
5. Record s_i(a), update m_i/r_i with bias correction, and slowly update tau_i.
6. Optional paired-view mode: for a small subset of steps or examples, draw K=2 views in the same batch to estimate within-step variance and calibrate the EMA estimate.
7. At evaluation time, report both model metrics and estimator diagnostics: hat_mu_i, hat_v_i, p_i, tau_i, and measured gradient-estimator variance.

### Datasets and models

- Clean sample-efficiency: CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 backbones following He et al. 2016 [18].
- Real label noise: CIFAR-10N and CIFAR-100N, using their human-noise annotations where available [7].
- Controlled label noise: CIFAR-10/100 with synthetic symmetric and asymmetric noise at 20 percent and 40 percent [20].
- Modality generality: SST-2 or another GLUE sentiment/classification task with token replacement, back-translation, mixup/manifold mixup, or transformer augmentation; use a BERT, DistilBERT, or TinyBERT-style encoder [8] [9] [13] [22].
- Optional scale check: tiny-ImageNet or an ImageNet subset with ResNet-50, included only if overhead remains modest [18] [19].

### Baselines

1. Uniform sampling plus the same standard augmentation policy.
2. Katharopoulos-Fleuret-style single-view gradient-norm or upper-bound importance sampling [2].
3. Self-paced or curriculum learning based on the same single-view score [5] [6].
4. Focal loss hard-example weighting [4].
5. MentorNet and/or co-teaching for noisy-label comparisons, with compute and auxiliary-network cost reported [6] [16].
6. Fixed AutoAugment, RandAugment, mixup, or comparable augmentation with uniform weighting [8] [10] [11] [12] [13].
7. AMI ablations: mu_i only (lambda=0, K or EMA > 1), v_i penalty only, no tau_i controller, EMA-only vs K=2 paired-view, and reciprocal penalty vs exponential penalty.

AMI is positioned as a strict wrapper that subsumes baselines 2-4 and interacts with 6: when K=1 and lambda=0, it collapses to the chosen single-view weighting rule; when tau_i is disabled, it tests weighting alone; when lambda is increased, it tests fragility suppression.

### Metrics

- Sample efficiency: accuracy as a function of gradient steps and examples seen.
- Final clean test accuracy.
- Robustness under real and synthetic label noise: test accuracy and noise memorization indicators.
- Direct theorem test: measured variance of the SGD gradient estimator under uniform, single-view IS, mu_i-only AMI, and full AMI. Estimate this by repeated minibatch draws at fixed checkpoints and report coordinate/projection variance or norm variance.
- Wall-clock, FLOPs, and memory overhead relative to single-view IS and uniform training.
- Augmentation-strength calibration: tau_i versus measured fragility, plus correlation between hat_v_i and held-out multi-view score variance.
- Stability diagnostics: effective sample size of p_i, maximum weight, and frequency of high-v_i down-weighting.
- Fairness/subgroup-safety diagnostics: for any available subgroup labels or proxies, report per-subgroup effective sample size under p_i, fraction down-weighted, subgroup composition among the most-down-weighted examples, disaggregated and worst-group accuracy, and lambda sensitivity for minority-group recall so fragility penalties cannot hide worst-group losses behind aggregate gains.

### Key experiments and ablations

1. Collapse test. Set K=1 and lambda=0, disable tau_i adaptation, use the exact same sampled augmentation and base score, and verify AMI weights match the single-view IS/focal/curriculum weights within floating-point tolerance. This is a unit test, not merely a plot.
2. Lambda and K sweep. Compare K=1, K=2, K=4 where feasible, and EMA-only horizons. Under weak augmentation, AMI should approach single-view IS; under strong augmentation, larger K or stable EMA should reduce estimator variance.
3. tau_i controller ablation. Compare full AMI to AMI with fixed tau and to tau_i-only without mu_i reweighting. This isolates whether gains come from marginal importance, fragility suppression, or policy adaptation.
4. Noisy-label robustness. Compare against MentorNet/co-teaching without giving AMI an auxiliary teacher. Report compute and memory cost honestly; the claim is lower machinery, not free superiority.
5. Gradient-variance measurement. At matched checkpoints, freeze theta, repeatedly sample minibatches/augmentations, and measure the empirical covariance predicted by the theorem: Cov_a(s_i(a), c_i(a)) and the variance of the resulting gradient estimator. AMI is supported only if reductions in this measured variance align with performance gains.
6. Confounding audit. Manually or programmatically categorize high-v_i examples: likely label noise, crop/augmentation harm, semantic ambiguity, or rare valid examples. This checks the limitation that v_i is useful but not causally pure.

Falsifiability triad:

1. Collapse: under K=1, lambda=0, and tau disabled, AMI must match the selected single-view baseline within floating-point tolerance.
2. Mechanism: under strong augmentation, AMI must reduce measured gradient-estimator variance relative to single-view IS.
3. Robustness: under label noise, AMI must match or beat MentorNet/co-teaching-style robustness with lower auxiliary-model compute; if it cannot, the teacher-free fragility claim is refuted.

### Expected outcomes

Expected directions, not claimed results: AMI should match or modestly beat single-view IS on clean CIFAR with small overhead when using EMA moments; gains should be larger under strong augmentation and label noise because the covariance/fragility term is larger; the tau_i controller should correlate with measured augmentation fragility; and AMI may approach MentorNet-level robustness without training a second network. These are bounded claims: if EMA non-stationarity, rare-example suppression, or augmentation-policy feedback dominate, the method can fail, and those failures are directly measurable by the ablations above.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] Needell, D.; Ward, R.; and Srebro, N. 2014. Stochastic Gradient Descent, Weighted Sampling, and the Randomized Kaczmarz Algorithm. NeurIPS.

[2] Katharopoulos, A. and Fleuret, F. 2018. Not All Samples Are Created Equal: Deep Learning with Importance Sampling. ICML.

[3] Freund, Y. and Schapire, R. E. 1997. A Decision-Theoretic Generalization of On-Line Learning and an Application to Boosting. Journal of Computer and System Sciences.

[4] Lin, T.-Y.; Goyal, P.; Girshick, R.; He, K.; and Dollar, P. 2017. Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection. ICCV.

[5] Jiang, L.; Meng, D.; Yu, S.-I.; Lan, Z.; Shan, S.; and Hauptmann, A. G. 2014. Self-Paced Learning with Diversity. NeurIPS.

[6] Jiang, L.; Zhou, Z.; Leung, T.; Li, L.-J.; and Fei-Fei, L. 2018. MentorNet: Learning Data-Driven Curriculum for Very Deep Neural Networks on Corrupted Labels. ICML.

[7] Jiang, L.; Huang, D.; Liu, M.; and Yang, W. 2020. Beyond Synthetic Noise: Deep Learning on Controlled Noisy Labels. ICML.

[8] Zhang, H.; Cisse, M.; Dauphin, Y. N.; and Lopez-Paz, D. 2018. mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization. ICLR.

[9] Guo, H.; Mao, Y.; and Zhang, R. 2019. Augmenting Data with Mixup for Sentence Classification: An Empirical Study. preprint.

[10] Cubuk, E. D.; Zoph, B.; Mane, D.; Vasudevan, V.; and Le, Q. V. 2019. AutoAugment: Learning Augmentation Strategies from Data. CVPR.

[11] Lim, S.; Kim, I.; Kim, T.; Kim, C.; and Kim, S. 2019. Fast AutoAugment. NeurIPS.

[12] Park, D. S.; Chan, W.; Zhang, Y.; Chiu, C.-C.; Zoph, B.; Cubuk, E. D.; and Le, Q. V. 2019. SpecAugment: A Simple Data Augmentation Method for Automatic Speech Recognition. Interspeech.

[13] Kumar, V.; Choudhary, A.; and Cho, E. 2020. Data Augmentation using Pre-trained Transformer Models. preprint.

[14] Madry, A.; Makelov, A.; Schmidt, L.; Tsipras, D.; and Vladu, A. 2018. Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks. ICLR.

[15] Jiang, H.; He, P.; Chen, W.; Liu, X.; Gao, J.; and Zhao, T. 2020. SMART: Robust and Efficient Fine-Tuning for Pre-trained Natural Language Models through Principled Regularized Optimization. ACL.

[16] Han, B.; Yao, Q.; Yu, X.; Niu, G.; Xu, M.; Hu, W.; Tsang, I.; and Sugiyama, M. 2018. Co-teaching: Robust Training of Deep Neural Networks with Extremely Noisy Labels. NeurIPS.

[17] Koh, P. W. and Liang, P. 2017. Understanding Black-box Predictions via Influence Functions. ICML.

[18] He, K.; Zhang, X.; Ren, S.; and Sun, J. 2016. Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition. CVPR.

[19] Krizhevsky, A.; Sutskever, I.; and Hinton, G. E. 2012. ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks. NeurIPS.

[20] Krizhevsky, A. 2009. Learning Multiple Layers of Features from Tiny Images. Technical report.

[21] Loshchilov, I. and Hutter, F. 2018. Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization. preprint.

[22] Jiao, X.; Yin, Y.; Shang, L.; Jiang, X.; Chen, X.; Li, L.; Wang, F.; and Liu, Q. 2019. TinyBERT: Distilling BERT for Natural Language Understanding. preprint.

[23] Goodfellow, I.; Bengio, Y.; and Courville, A. 2016. Deep Learning. MIT Press.
